Campaign 'Cutting Edge': During Cutting Edge, threat actors exploited CVE-2023-46805 and CVE-2024-21887 in Ivanti Connect Secure VPN appliances to enable authentication bypass and command injection. A server-side request forgery (SSRF) vulnerability, CVE-2024-21893, was identified later and used to bypass mitigations for the initial two vulnerabilities by chaining with CVE-2024-21887.
Campaign 'FLORAHOX Activity': FLORAHOX Activity has exploited and infected vulnerable routers to recruit additional network devices into the ORB.
Campaign 'ArcaneDoor': ArcaneDoor abused WebVPN traffic to targeted devices to achieve unauthorized remote code execution.
Campaign 'FrostyGoop Incident': FrostyGoop Incident was likely enabled by the adversary exploiting an unknown vulnerability in an external-facing router.

T1046.name: Network Service Discovery
T1046.description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of services running on remote hosts and local network infrastructure devices, including those that may be vulnerable to remote software exploitation. Common methods to acquire this information include port, vulnerability, and/or wordlist scans using tools that are brought onto a system.(Citation: CISA AR21-126A FIVEHANDS May 2021) Within cloud environments, adversaries may attempt to discover services running on other cloud hosts. Additionally, if the cloud environment is connected to a on-premises environment, adversaries may be able to identify services running on non-cloud systems as well. Within macOS environments, adversaries may use the native Bonjour application to discover services running on other macOS hosts within a network. The Bonjour mDNSResponder daemon automatically registers and advertises a host’s registered services on the network. For example, adversaries can use a mDNS query (such as dns-sd -B _ssh._tcp .) to find other systems broadcasting the ssh service.(Citation: apple doco bonjour description)(Citation: macOS APT Activity Bradley)
T1046.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Disable or Remove Feature or Program': Ensure that unnecessary ports and services are closed to prevent risk of discovery and potential exploitation.
Mitigation 'Network Intrusion Prevention': Use network intrusion detection/prevention systems to detect and prevent remote service scans.
Mitigation 'Network Segmentation': Ensure proper network segmentation is followed to protect critical servers and devices.
T1046.detections:
Detection Strategy: Behavioral Detection Strategy for Network Service Discovery Across Platforms
- Analytic 'Analytic 1057': Detects processes performing network enumeration (e.g., port scans, service probing) by correlating process creation, socket connections, and sequential destination IP probing within a time window.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1058': Detects use of network scanning utilities or scripts performing rapid connections to multiple services or hosts using auditd and netflow/pcap telemetry.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1059': Detects Bonjour-based mDNS enumeration or use of system tools (e.g., dns-sd, nmap) to find active services via multicast probing or targeted scans.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1060': Detects lateral discovery or container breakout attempts using netcat, curl, or custom binaries probing other services within the same namespace or VPC subnet.
T1046.procedures: Software 'Pupy': Pupy has a built-in module for port scanning. Software 'Empire': Empire can perform port scans from an infected host. Software 'NBTscan': NBTscan can be used to scan IP networks. Software 'Peirates': Peirates can initiate a port scan against a given IP address. Software 'Koadic': Koadic can scan for open TCP ports on the target network. Software 'FRP': As part of load balancing FRP can set `healthCheck.type = "tcp"` or `healthCheck.type = "http"` to check service status on specific hosts with TCPing or an HTTP request. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can scan for open ports on a compromised machine. Software 'Brute Ratel C4': Brute Ratel C4 can conduct port scanning against targeted systems. Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 can perform port scans from an infected host. Software 'Backdoor.Oldrea': Backdoor.Oldrea can use a network scanning module to identify ICS-related ports. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer uses a custom port scanner to map out a network. Software 'Ramsay': Ramsay can scan for systems that are vulnerable to the EternalBlue exploit. Software 'Royal': Royal can scan the network interfaces of targeted systems. Software 'SpeakUp': SpeakUp checks for availability of specific ports on servers. Software 'BlackEnergy': BlackEnergy has conducted port scans on a host. Software 'Hildegard': Hildegard has used masscan to look for kubelets in the internal Kubernetes network. Software 'BADHATCH': BADHATCH can check for open ports on a computer by establishing a TCP connection. Software 'ZxShell': ZxShell can launch port scans. Software 'China Chopper': China Chopper's server component can spider authentication portals. Software 'BlackByte Ransomware': BlackByte Ransomware identifies remote systems via active directory queries for hostnames prior to launching remote ransomware payloads. Software 'Xbash': Xbash can perform port scanning of TCP and UDP ports. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can perform port scans from an infected host. Software 'P.A.S. Webshell': P.A.S. Webshell can scan networks for open ports and listening services. Software 'XTunnel': XTunnel is capable of probing the network for open ports. Software 'HermeticWizard': HermeticWizard has the ability to scan ports on a compromised network. Software 'MgBot': MgBot includes modules for performing HTTP and server service scans. Software 'LightSpy': To collect data on the host's Wi-Fi connection history, LightSpy reads the `/Library/Preferences/SystemConfiguration/com.apple.airport.preferences.plist file`.It also utilizes Apple's CWWiFiClient API to scan for nearby Wi-Fi networks and obtain data on the SSID, security type, and RSSI (signal strength) values. Software 'Conficker': Conficker scans for other machines to infect. Software 'HDoor': HDoor scans to identify open ports on the victim. Software 'MURKYTOP': MURKYTOP has the capability to scan for open ports on hosts in a connected network. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can scan the network for open ports and vulnerable instances of RDP and SMB protocols. Software 'Remsec': Remsec has a plugin that can perform ARP scanning as well as port scanning. Software 'Lucifer': Lucifer can scan for open ports including TCP ports 135 and 1433. Software 'Caterpillar WebShell': Caterpillar WebShell has a module to use a port scanner on a system. Software 'Pysa': Pysa can perform network reconnaissance using the Advanced Port Scanner tool. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used commercial tools, LOTL utilities, and appliances already present on the system for network service discovery. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used CrackMapExec and a custom port scanner known as BLUETORCH for network scanning. Group 'BlackTech': BlackTech has used the SNScan tool to find other potential targets on victim networks. Group 'BlackByte': BlackByte has used tools such as NetScan to enumerate network services in victim environments. Group 'menuPass': menuPass has used tcping.exe, similar to Ping, to probe port status on systems of interest. Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 actors use the Hunter tool to conduct network service discovery for vulnerable systems. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has leveraged NBTscan to scan IP networks. Group 'Agrius': Agrius used the open-source port scanner WinEggDrop to perform detailed scans of hosts of interest in victim networks. Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has utilized `nmap` for reconnaissance efforts. FIN13 has also scanned for internal MS-SQL servers in a compromised network. Group 'DarkVishnya': DarkVishnya performed port scanning to obtain the list of active services. Group 'OilRig': OilRig has used the publicly available tool SoftPerfect Network Scanner as well as a custom tool called GOLDIRONY to conduct network scanning. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group has used nmap from a router VM to scan ports on systems within the restricted segment of an enterprise network. Group 'Leafminer': Leafminer scanned network services to search for vulnerabilities in the victim system. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper used pr and an openly available tool to scan for open ports on target systems. Group 'Naikon': Naikon has used the LadonGo scanner to scan target networks. Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound has used KPortScan 3.0 to perform SMB, RDP, and LDAP scanning. Group 'Rocke': Rocke conducted scanning for exposed TCP port 7001 as well as SSH and Redis servers. Group 'APT32': APT32 performed network scanning on the network to search for open ports, services, OS finger-printing, and other vulnerabilities. Group 'INC Ransom': INC Ransom has used NETSCAN.EXE for internal reconnaissance. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has used the get -b -e -p command for network scanning as well as a custom Python tool packed into a Windows executable named Get.exe to scan IP ranges for HTTP. Group 'APT41': APT41 used a malware variant called WIDETONE to conduct port scans on specified subnets. Group 'Suckfly': Suckfly the victim's internal network for hosts with ports 8080, 5900, and 40 open. Group 'FIN6': FIN6 used publicly available tools (including Microsoft's built-in SQL querying tool, osql.exe) to map the internal network and conduct reconnaissance against Active Directory, Structured Query Language (SQL) servers, and NetBIOS. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has used masscan to search for open Docker API ports and Kubernetes clusters. TeamTNT has also used malware that utilizes zmap and zgrab to search for vulnerable services in cloud environments. Group 'BackdoorDiplomacy': BackdoorDiplomacy has used SMBTouch, a vulnerability scanner, to determine whether a target is vulnerable to EternalBlue malware. Group 'Lotus Blossom': Lotus Blossom has used port scanners to enumerate services on remote hosts. Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has used tools including NMAP to conduct broad scanning to identify open ports. Group 'Medusa Group': Medusa Group has the capability to use living off the land (LOTL) binaries to perform network enumeration. Medusa Group has also utilized the publicly available scanning tool SoftPerfect Network Scanner (`netscan.exe`) to discover device hostnames and network services. Group 'Cobalt Group': Cobalt Group leveraged an open-source tool called SoftPerfect Network Scanner to perform network scanning. Group 'RedCurl': RedCurl has used netstat to check if port 4119 is open. Group 'Ember Bear': Ember Bear has used tools such as NMAP for remote system discovery and enumeration in victim environments. Group 'VOID MANTICORE': During HomeLand Justice, threat actors executed the Advanced Port Scanner tool on compromised systems. Group 'Scattered Spider': During C0027, used RustScan to scan for open ports on targeted ESXi appliances. Campaign 'CostaRicto': During CostaRicto, the threat actors employed nmap and pscan to scan target environments. Campaign 'HomeLand Justice': During HomeLand Justice, threat actors executed the Advanced Port Scanner tool on compromised systems. Campaign 'C0027': During C0027, used RustScan to scan for open ports on targeted ESXi appliances. Campaign 'C0018': During C0018, the threat actors used the SoftPerfect Network Scanner for network scanning. Campaign 'Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign': During the Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign, the adversary used Claude Code to enumerate internal network services and endpoints across targeted environments using browser automation via MCP, including databases, container registries, admin interfaces, and workflow orchestration platforms. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors scanned for open ports and used nbtscan to find NETBIOS nameservers. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries utilized Ping, the Advanced Port Scanner and Advanced IP Scanner to enumerate network devices.

T1210.name: Exploitation of Remote Services
T1210.description:
Adversaries may exploit remote services to gain unauthorized access to internal systems once inside of a network. Exploitation of a software vulnerability occurs when an adversary takes advantage of a programming error in a program, service, or within the operating system software or kernel itself to execute adversary-controlled code. A common goal for post-compromise exploitation of remote services is for lateral movement to enable access to a remote system.

An adversary may need to determine if the remote system is in a vulnerable state, which may be done through [Network Service Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1046) or other Discovery methods looking for common, vulnerable software that may be deployed in the network, the lack of certain patches that may indicate vulnerabilities,  or security software that may be used to detect or contain remote exploitation. Servers are likely a high value target for lateral movement exploitation, but endpoint systems may also be at risk if they provide an advantage or access to additional resources.

There are several well-known vulnerabilities that exist in common services such as SMB(Citation: CIS Multiple SMB Vulnerabilities) and RDP(Citation: NVD CVE-2017-0176) as well as applications that may be used within internal networks such as MySQL(Citation: NVD CVE-2016-6662) and web server services.(Citation: NVD CVE-2014-7169)(Citation: Ars Technica VMWare Code Execution Vulnerability 2021) Additionally, there have been a number of vulnerabilities in VMware vCenter installations, which may enable threat actors to move laterally from the compromised vCenter server to virtual machines or even to ESXi hypervisors.(Citation: Broadcom VMSA-2024-0019)

Depending on the permissions level of the vulnerable remote service an adversary may achieve [Exploitation for Privilege Escalation](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1068) as a result of lateral movement exploitation as well.
T1210.mitigations:
Mitigation 'Disable or Remove Feature or Program': Minimize available services to only those that are necessary.
Mitigation 'Vulnerability Scanning': Regularly scan the internal network for available services to identify new and potentially vulnerable services.
Mitigation 'Exploit Protection': Security applications that look for behavior used during exploitation such as Windows Defender Exploit Guard (WDEG) and the Enhanced Mitigation Experience Toolkit (EMET) can be used to mitigate some exploitation behavior. Control flow integrity checking is another way to potentially identify and stop a software exploit from occurring. Many of these protections depend on the architecture and target application binary for compatibility and may not work for all software or services targeted.
Mitigation 'Network Segmentation': Segment networks and systems appropriately to reduce access to critical systems and services to controlled methods.
Mitigation 'Threat Intelligence Program': Develop a robust cyber threat intelligence capability to determine what types and levels of threat may use software exploits and 0-days against a particular organization.
Mitigation 'Application Isolation and Sandboxing': Make it difficult for adversaries to advance their operation through exploitation of undiscovered or unpatched vulnerabilities by using sandboxing. Other types of virtualization and application microsegmentation may also mitigate the impact of some types of exploitation. Risks of additional exploits and weaknesses in these systems may still exist.
Mitigation 'Privileged Account Management': Minimize permissions and access for service accounts to limit impact of exploitation.
Mitigation 'Update Software': Update software regularly by employing patch management for internal enterprise endpoints and servers.
T1210.detections:
Detection Strategy: Exploitation of Remote Services – multi-platform lateral movement detection
- Analytic 'Analytic 0327': Correlates inbound network access to remote service ports (e.g., SMB/RPC 445/135, RDP 3389, WinRM 5985/5986) with near-time instability in the target service (crash, abnormal restart), suspicious child process creation under the service, and post-access lateral-movement behaviors. The chain indicates likely exploitation rather than normal administration.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0328': Links inbound network access to SSHD/SMB/NFS/Databases or custom daemons with subsequent daemon crash/restart, core dump, or spawning of shells/reverse shells from the service context, indicating remote exploitation.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0329': Detects exploitation targeting ESXi/vCenter by correlating attempts to reach known exploitable endpoints (OpenSLP 427, CIM 5989, Hostd/Vpxa HTTPS 443, ESXi SOAP) with vmkernel/hostd crashes, unexpected hostd/vpxa restarts, or new reverse/outbound connections from ESXi host/vCenter to internal assets.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0330': Ties inbound access to exposed services (ARD/VNC 5900, SSH 22, ScreenSharing, web services) with process crashes in unified logs and abnormal child processes spawned under those services (e.g., bash, curl) to indicate exploitation.
T1210.procedures:
Software 'Empire': Empire has a limited number of built-in modules for exploiting remote SMB, JBoss, and Jenkins servers.
Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 contains a module for exploiting SMB via EternalBlue.
Software 'Flame': Flame can use MS10-061 to exploit a print spooler vulnerability in a remote system with a shared printer in order to move laterally.
Software 'WannaCry': WannaCry uses an exploit in SMBv1 to spread itself to other remote systems on a network.
Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet propagates using the MS10-061 Print Spooler and MS08-067 Windows Server Service vulnerabilities.
Software 'QakBot': QakBot can move laterally using worm-like functionality through exploitation of SMB.
Software 'Emotet': Emotet has been seen exploiting SMB via a vulnerability exploit like EternalBlue (MS17-010) to achieve lateral movement and propagation.
Software 'Bad Rabbit': Bad Rabbit used the EternalRomance SMB exploit to spread through victim networks.
Software 'NotPetya': NotPetya can use two exploits in SMBv1, EternalBlue and EternalRomance, to spread itself to other remote systems on the network.
Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can spread within a network via the BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) and EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144) vulnerabilities in RDP and SMB respectively.
Software 'Conficker': Conficker exploited the MS08-067 Windows vulnerability for remote code execution through a crafted RPC request.
Software 'Lucifer': Lucifer can exploit multiple vulnerabilities including EternalBlue (CVE-2017-0144) and EternalRomance (CVE-2017-0144).
Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot utilizes EternalBlue and EternalRomance exploits for lateral movement in the modules wormwinDll, wormDll, mwormDll, nwormDll, tabDll.
Group 'Wizard Spider': Wizard Spider has exploited or attempted to exploit Zerologon (CVE-2020-1472) and EternalBlue (MS17-010) vulnerabilities.
Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has exploited known vulnerabilities in remote services including RDP.
Group 'Earth Lusca': Earth Lusca has used Mimikatz to exploit a domain controller via the ZeroLogon exploit (CVE-2020-1472).
Group 'Ember Bear': Ember Bear has used exploits for vulnerabilities such as MS17-010, also known as `Eternal Blue`, during operations.
Group 'APT28': APT28 exploited a Windows SMB Remote Code Execution Vulnerability to conduct lateral movement.
Group 'Tonto Team': Tonto Team has used EternalBlue exploits for lateral movement.
Group 'menuPass': menuPass has used tools to exploit the ZeroLogon vulnerability (CVE-2020-1472).
Group 'Dragonfly': Dragonfly has exploited a Windows Netlogon vulnerability (CVE-2020-1472) to obtain access to Windows Active Directory servers.
Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has exploited the Microsoft Netlogon vulnerability (CVE-2020-1472).
Group 'Threat Group-3390': Threat Group-3390 has exploited MS17-010 to move laterally to other systems on the network.
Group 'FIN7': FIN7 has exploited ZeroLogon (CVE-2020-1472) against vulnerable domain controllers.

T1588.006.name: Vulnerabilities
T1588.006.description:
Adversaries may acquire information about vulnerabilities that can be used during targeting. A vulnerability is a weakness in computer hardware or software that can, potentially, be exploited by an adversary to cause unintended or unanticipated behavior to occur. Adversaries may find vulnerability information by searching open databases or gaining access to closed vulnerability databases.(Citation: National Vulnerability Database)

An adversary may monitor vulnerability disclosures/databases to understand the state of existing, as well as newly discovered, vulnerabilities. There is usually a delay between when a vulnerability is discovered and when it is made public. An adversary may target the systems of those known to conduct vulnerability research (including commercial vendors). Knowledge of a vulnerability may cause an adversary to search for an existing exploit (i.e. [Exploits](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1588/005)) or to attempt to develop one themselves (i.e. [Exploits](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1587/004)).
T1588.006.mitigations: Mitigation 'Pre-compromise': This technique cannot be easily mitigated with preventive controls since it is based on behaviors performed outside of the scope of enterprise defenses and controls.
T1588.006.detections:
Detection Strategy: Detection of Vulnerabilities
- Analytic 'Analytic 1940': Much of this activity will take place outside the visibility of the target organization, making detection of this behavior difficult. Detection efforts may be focused on behaviors relating to the potential use of exploits for vulnerabilities (i.e. Exploit Public-Facing Application, Exploitation for Client Execution, Exploitation for Privilege Escalation, Exploitation for Stealth, Exploitation for Credential Access, Exploitation of Remote Services, and Application or System Exploitation).
T1588.006.procedures:
Group 'Sandworm Team': In 2017, Sandworm Team conducted technical research related to vulnerabilities associated with websites used by the Korean Sport and Olympic Committee, a Korean power company, and a Korean airport.
Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has used publicly available exploit code for initial access.
Group 'Storm-0501': Storm-0501 has obtained capabilities to exploit N-day vulnerabilities associated with public facing services to gain initial access to victim environments to include Zoho ManageEngine (CVE-2022-47966), Citrix NetScaler “Citrix Bleed” (CVE-2023-4966), and Adobe ColdFusion 2016 (CVE-2023-29300 or CVE-2023-38203).
Group 'Leviathan': Leviathan Australian Intrusions was conducted by the Leviathan threat actor.Leviathan weaponized publicly-known vulnerabilities for initial access and other purposes during Leviathan Australian Intrusions.
Campaign 'Leviathan Australian Intrusions': Leviathan weaponized publicly-known vulnerabilities for initial access and other purposes during Leviathan Australian Intrusions.

CVE-2024-4577.title: PHP-CGI OS Command Injection Vulnerability
CVE-2024-4577.description: In PHP versions 8.1.* before 8.1.29, 8.2.* before 8.2.20, 8.3.* before 8.3.8, when using Apache and PHP-CGI on Windows, if the system is set up to use certain code pages, Windows may use "Best-Fit" behavior to replace characters in command line given to Win32 API functions. PHP CGI module may misinterpret those characters as PHP options, which may allow a malicious user to pass options to PHP binary being run, and thus reveal the source code of scripts, run arbitrary PHP code on the server, etc.
CVE-2024-4577.mitigations: Apply mitigations per vendor instructions or discontinue use of the product if mitigations are unavailable. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2012-1823.title: PHP-CGI Query String Parameter Vulnerability
CVE-2012-1823.description: sapi/cgi/cgi_main.c in PHP before 5.3.12 and 5.4.x before 5.4.2, when configured as a CGI script (aka php-cgi), does not properly handle query strings that lack an = (equals sign) character, which allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code by placing command-line options in the query string, related to lack of skipping a certain php_getopt for the 'd' case.
CVE-2012-1823.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

CVE-2019-11043.title: PHP FastCGI Process Manager (FPM) Buffer Overflow Vulnerability
CVE-2019-11043.description: In PHP versions 7.1.x below 7.1.33, 7.2.x below 7.2.24 and 7.3.x below 7.3.11 in certain configurations of FPM setup it is possible to cause FPM module to write past allocated buffers into the space reserved for FCGI protocol data, thus opening the possibility of remote code execution.
CVE-2019-11043.mitigations: Apply updates per vendor instructions. Not mitigations found in references.

mitre_evaluations.explanation: The alert shows a request from 'Nikto', a well-known web vulnerability scanner, targeting a specific PHP CGI-bin path. This is a textbook example of vulnerability scanning to identify exploitable software versions or configurations.

The alert describes an attempt to exploit a public-facing web application (PHP CGI-bin). The presence of a scanner (Nikto) indicates an attempt to find a weakness in an internet-facing host to gain initial access.

While the primary action is a vulnerability scan (T1595.002), the use of a tool like Nikto is also a method of service discovery to identify what services and versions are running on the target web server.

The alert targets a remote service (PHP-CGI). While the log shows a scan/attempt rather than a confirmed successful lateral movement, the intent of the attack is to exploit a remote service to gain access.

The attacker is using a scanner to gather information about specific vulnerabilities (PHP-CGI) that can be exploited. This falls under the reconnaissance/resource development phase of identifying weaknesses.

cve_evaluations.explanation: This is a recent PHP-CGI command injection vulnerability. While the alert is for a PHP-CGI attempt, the log shows a Nikto scan which is often generic. Without knowing the specific PHP version on the server, this is a possible but unconfirmed target.

This CVE is highly relevant as it specifically addresses a PHP-CGI vulnerability involving query strings that lack an '=' character. The alert log shows a request to '/?-s', which matches the pattern of attempting to pass command-line options via the query string.

While this is a PHP-related vulnerability, it specifically targets the FastCGI Process Manager (FPM) buffer overflow. The alert is more directly aligned with the older CGI-bin style exploits (like CVE-2012-1823) rather than FPM-specific memory corruption.

rule.description: Executed recursive query of all files using ls command.

mitre.description: The alert identifies the execution of the '/usr/bin/ls' command with arguments designed to perform a recursive search across system paths (including /sys, /system, and /private/var) by the 'www-data' user. The presence of the audit key 'T1166_Seuid_and_Setgid' suggests this activity is associated with MITRE ATT&CK technique T1166 (Search Victim-Owned Files and Directories) or general discovery behavior following a potential privilege escalation or unauthorized access attempt via a web service user.
mitre.keywords: T1166, Discovery, File and Directory Discovery

cve.description: No specific software vulnerabilities or CVE identifiers were mentioned in the alert.

T1083.name: File and Directory Discovery
T1083.description: Adversaries may enumerate files and directories or may search in specific locations of a host or network share for certain information within a file system. Adversaries may use the information from [File and Directory Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1083) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions. Many command shell utilities can be used to obtain this information. Examples include dir, tree, ls, find, and locate.(Citation: Windows Commands JPCERT) Custom tools may also be used to gather file and directory information and interact with the [Native API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1106). Adversaries may also leverage a [Network Device CLI](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/008) on network devices to gather file and directory information (e.g. dir, show flash, and/or nvram).(Citation: US-CERT-TA18-106A) Some files and directories may require elevated or specific user permissions to access.
T1083.detections:
Detection Strategy: Recursive Enumeration of Files and Directories Across Privilege Contexts
- Analytic 'Analytic 1040': Execution of file enumeration commands (e.g., 'dir', 'tree') from non-standard processes or unusual user contexts, followed by recursive directory traversal or access to sensitive locations.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1041': Use of file enumeration commands (e.g., 'ls', 'find', 'locate') executed by suspicious users or scripts accessing broad file hierarchies or restricted directories.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1042': Execution of file or directory discovery commands (e.g., 'ls', 'find') from terminal or script-based tooling, especially outside normal user workflows.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1043': Execution of esxcli commands to enumerate datastore, configuration files, or directory structures by unauthorized or remote users.
- Analytic 'Analytic 1044': Execution of file discovery commands (e.g., 'dir', 'show flash', 'nvram:') from CLI interfaces, especially by unauthorized users or from abnormal source IPs.
T1083.procedures: Software 'Sliver': Sliver can enumerate files on a target system. Software 'cmd': cmd can be used to find files and directories with native functionality such as dir commands. Software 'Rclone': Rclone can list files and directories with the `ls`, `lsd`, and `lsl` commands. Software 'Empire': Empire includes various modules for finding files of interest on hosts and network shares. Software 'Pupy': Pupy can walk through directories and recursively search for strings in files. Software 'PoshC2': PoshC2 can enumerate files on the local file system and includes a module for enumerating recently accessed files. Software 'Koadic': Koadic can obtain a list of directories. Software 'Forfiles': Forfiles can be used to locate certain types of files/directories in a system.(ex: locate all files with a specific extension, name, and/or age) Software 'CrackMapExec': CrackMapExec can discover specified filetypes and log files on a targeted system. Software 'TruffleHog': TruffleHog has can browse and scan individual files and directories. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY has several modules, such as `ls.py`, `pwd.py`, and `recentFiles.py`, to enumerate directories and files. Software 'RemoteUtilities': RemoteUtilities can enumerate files and directories on a target machine. Software 'Remcos': Remcos can search for files on the infected machine. Software 'Imminent Monitor': Imminent Monitor has a dynamic debugging feature to check whether it is located in the %TEMP% directory, otherwise it copies itself there. Software 'Diskpart': If executed with elevated privileges, Diskpart can list all volumes, including virtual disks. Software 'BLACKCOFFEE': BLACKCOFFEE has the capability to enumerate files. Software 'Orz': Orz can gather victim drive information. Software 'Attor': Attor has a plugin that enumerates files with specific extensions on all hard disk drives and stores file information in encrypted log files. Software 'USBStealer': USBStealer searches victim drives for files matching certain extensions (“.skr”,“.pkr” or “.key”) or names. Software 'SDBbot': SDBbot has the ability to get directory listings or drive information on a compromised host. Software 'Kinsing': Kinsing has used the find command to search for specific files. Software 'Amadey': Amadey has searched for folders associated with antivirus software. Software 'Woody RAT': Woody RAT can list all files and their associated attributes, including filename, type, owner, creation time, last access time, last write time, size, and permissions. Software 'PlugX': PlugX has a module to enumerate drives and find files recursively. PlugX has also checked the path from which it is running for specific parameters prior to execution. Software 'Akira': Akira examines files prior to encryption to determine if they meet requirements for encryption and can be encrypted by the ransomware. These checks are performed through native Windows functions such as GetFileAttributesW. Software 'Volgmer': Volgmer can list directories on a victim. Software 'AshTag': The AshTag AshenOrchestrator component can enumerate files on victim hosts. Software 'P.A.S. Webshell': P.A.S. Webshell has the ability to list files and file characteristics including extension, size, ownership, and permissions. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can enumerate the victim's desktop. Software 'Crimson': Crimson contains commands to list files and directories, as well as search for files matching certain extensions from a defined list. Software 'SynAck': SynAck checks its directory location in an attempt to avoid launching in a sandbox. Software 'yty': yty gathers information on victim’s drives and has a plugin for document listing. Software 'TAINTEDSCRIBE': TAINTEDSCRIBE can use DirectoryList to enumerate files in a specified directory. Software 'ELMER': ELMER is capable of performing directory listings. Software 'SUGARDUMP': SUGARDUMP can search for and collect data from specific Chrome, Opera, Microsoft Edge, and Firefox files, including any folders that have the string `Profile` in its name. Software 'Fysbis': Fysbis has the ability to search for files. Software 'Remsec': Remsec is capable of listing contents of folders on the victim. Remsec also searches for custom network encryption software on victims. Software 'KillDisk': KillDisk has used the FindNextFile command as part of its file deletion process. Software 'InvisibleFerret': InvisibleFerret has identified specific directories and files for exfiltration using the `ssh_upload` command which contains subcommands of `.sdira`, `sdir`, `sfile`, `sfinda`, `sfindr`, `sfind`. InvisibleFerret also has the capability to scan and upload files of interest from multiple OS systems through the use of scripts that check file names, file extensions, and avoids certain path names. InvisibleFerret has utilized the `findstr` on Windows or the macOS `find` commands to search for files of interest. Software 'Embargo': Embargo has searched for folders, subfolders and other networked or mounted drives for follow on encryption actions. Embargo has also iterated device volumes using `FindFirstVolumeW()` and `FindNextVolumeW()` functions and then calls the `GetVolumePathNamesForVolumeNameW()` function to retrieve a list of drive letters and mounted folder paths for each specified volume. Software 'WinMM': WinMM sets a WH_CBT Windows hook to search for and capture files on the victim. Software 'Turian': Turian can search for specific files and list directories. Software 'POORAIM': POORAIM can conduct file browsing. Software 'Micropsia': Micropsia can perform a recursive directory listing for all volume drives available on the victim's machine and can also fetch specific files by their paths. Software 'Misdat': Misdat is capable of running commands to obtain a list of files and directories, as well as enumerating logical drives. Software 'Winnti for Windows': Winnti for Windows can check for the presence of specific files prior to moving to the next phase of execution. Software 'GeminiDuke': GeminiDuke collects information from the victim, including installed drivers, programs previously executed by users, programs and services configured to automatically run at startup, files and folders present in any user's home folder, files and folders present in any user's My Documents, programs installed to the Program Files folder, and recently accessed files, folders, and programs. Software 'BoxCaon': BoxCaon has searched for files on the system, such as documents located in the desktop folder. Software 'LazyWiper': LazyWiper can specifically target multiple files by extension including: .rar, .tar.gz, .zip, .7z, .json, .bcp, .bak, .gho, .erf, .edb, .onepkg, .pst, and .ldiff. Software 'njRAT': njRAT can browse file systems using a file manager module. Software 'PinchDuke': PinchDuke searches for files created within a certain timeframe and whose file extension matches a predefined list. Software 'WindTail': WindTail has the ability to enumerate the users home directory and the path to its own application bundle. Software 'AuditCred': AuditCred can search through folders and files on the system. Software 'Diavol': Diavol has a command to traverse the files and directories in a given path. Software 'PowerDuke': PowerDuke has commands to get the current directory name as well as the size of a file. It also has commands to obtain information about logical drives, drive type, and free space. Software 'BackConfig': BackConfig has the ability to identify folders and files related to previous infections. Software 'Bankshot': Bankshot searches for files on the victim's machine. Software 'PingPull': PingPull can enumerate storage volumes and folder contents of a compromised host. Software '4H RAT': 4H RAT has the capability to obtain file and directory listings. Software 'FALLCHILL': FALLCHILL can search files on a victim. Software 'MarkiRAT': MarkiRAT can look for files carrying specific extensions such as: .rtf, .doc, .docx, .xls, .xlsx, .ppt, .pptx, .pps, .ppsx, .txt, .gpg, .pkr, .kdbx, .key, and .jpb. Software 'Epic': Epic recursively searches for all .doc files on the system and collects a directory listing of the Desktop, %TEMP%, and %WINDOWS%\Temp directories. Software 'Clambling': Clambling can browse directories on a compromised host. Software 'down_new': down_new has the ability to list the directories on a compromised host. Software 'Ryuk': Ryuk has enumerated files and folders on all mounted drives. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can identify whether it has been run previously on a host by checking for a specified folder. Software 'PoetRAT': PoetRAT has the ability to list files upon receiving the ls command from C2. Software 'WhisperGate': WhisperGate can locate files based on hardcoded file extensions. Software 'Seasalt': Seasalt has the capability to identify the drive type on a victim. Software 'KEYMARBLE': KEYMARBLE has a command to search for files on the victim’s machine. Software 'SHOTPUT': SHOTPUT has a command to obtain a directory listing. Software 'WINERACK': WINERACK can enumerate files and directories. Software 'Cardinal RAT': Cardinal RAT checks its current working directory upon execution and also contains watchdog functionality that ensures its executable is located in the correct path (else it will rewrite the payload). Software 'OwaAuth': OwaAuth has a command to list its directory and logical drives. Software 'RARSTONE': RARSTONE obtains installer properties from Uninstall Registry Key entries to obtain information about installed applications and how to uninstall certain applications. Software 'ANELLDR': ANELLDR can enumerate files in the current directory to search for encrypted payload files. Software 'Kivars': Kivars has the ability to list drives on the infected host. Software 'BlackEnergy': BlackEnergy gathers a list of installed apps from the uninstall program Registry. It also gathers registered mail, browser, and instant messaging clients from the Registry. BlackEnergy has searched for given file types. Software 'NightClub': NightClub can use a file monitor to identify .lnk, .doc, .docx, .xls, .xslx, and .pdf files. Software 'LunarWeb': LunarWeb has the ability to retrieve directory listings. Software 'WannaCry': WannaCry searches for variety of user files by file extension before encrypting them using RSA and AES, including Office, PDF, image, audio, video, source code, archive/compression format, and key and certificate files. Software 'SoreFang': SoreFang has the ability to list directories. Software 'AutoIt backdoor': AutoIt backdoor is capable of identifying documents on the victim with the following extensions: .doc; .pdf, .csv, .ppt, .docx, .pst, .xls, .xlsx, .pptx, and .jpeg. Software 'Caterpillar WebShell': Caterpillar WebShell can search for files in directories. Software 'USBferry': USBferry can detect the victim's file or folder list. Software 'Avaddon': Avaddon has searched for specific files prior to encryption. Software 'SideTwist': SideTwist has the ability to search for specific files. Software 'GrimAgent': GrimAgent has the ability to enumerate files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'Skidmap': Skidmap has checked for the existence of specific files including /usr/sbin/setenforce and /etc/selinux/config. It also has the ability to monitor the cryptocurrency miner file and process. Software 'zwShell': zwShell can browse the file system. Software 'ZxShell': ZxShell has a command to open a file manager and explorer on the system. Software 'CHIMNEYSWEEP': CHIMNEYSWEEP has the ability to enumerate directories for files that match a set list. Software 'DropBook': DropBook can collect the names of all files and folders in the Program Files directories. Software 'TSCookie': TSCookie has the ability to discover drive information on the infected host. Software 'Kwampirs': Kwampirs collects a list of files and directories in C:\ with the command dir /s /a c:\ >> "C:\windows\TEMP\[RANDOM].tmp". Software 'Ninja': Ninja has the ability to enumerate directory content. Software 'JPIN': JPIN can enumerate drives and their types. It can also change file permissions using cacls.exe. Software 'ZLib': ZLib has the ability to enumerate files and drives. Software 'FLASHFLOOD': FLASHFLOOD searches for interesting files (either a default or customized set of file extensions) on the local system and removable media. Software 'Lokibot': Lokibot can search for specific files on an infected host. Software 'HOPLIGHT': HOPLIGHT has been observed enumerating system drives and partitions. Software 'Mango': Mango can enumerate the contents of current working or other specified directories. Software 'BadPatch': BadPatch searches for files with specific file extensions. Software 'NETEAGLE': NETEAGLE allows adversaries to enumerate and modify the infected host's file system. It supports searching for directories, creating directories, listing directory contents, reading and writing to files, retrieving file attributes, and retrieving volume information. Software 'SysUpdate': SysUpdate can search files on a compromised host. Software 'Latrodectus': Latrodectus can collect desktop filenames. Software 'LockBit 3.0': LockBit 3.0 can exclude files associated with core system functions from encryption. Software 'BabyShark': BabyShark has used dir to search for "programfiles" and "appdata". Software 'MoonWind': MoonWind has a command to return a directory listing for a specified directory. Software 'MegaCortex': MegaCortex can parse the available drives and directories to determine which files to encrypt. Software 'Cuckoo Stealer': Cuckoo Stealer can search for files associated with specific applications. Software 'Pteranodon': Pteranodon identifies files matching certain file extension and copies them to subdirectories it created. Software 'CookieMiner': CookieMiner has looked for files in the user's home directory with "wallet" in their name using find. Software 'DynoWiper': DynoWiper has used the Microsoft Windows native `FindFirstFile()` and `FindNextFile()` to recursively enumerate directories and files on the system. Software 'CrossRAT': CrossRAT can list all files on a system. Software 'DEATHRANSOM': DEATHRANSOM can use loop operations to enumerate directories on a compromised host. Software 'InnaputRAT': InnaputRAT enumerates directories and obtains file attributes on a system. Software 'SUNBURST': SUNBURST had commands to enumerate files and directories. Software 'BOLDMOVE': BOLDMOVE can list information of all files in the system recursively from the root directory or from a specified directory. Software 'Cuba': Cuba can enumerate files by using a variety of functions. Software 'Linfo': Linfo creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can list contents of drives and search for files. Software 'CosmicDuke': CosmicDuke searches attached and mounted drives for file extensions and keywords that match a predefined list. Software 'Spica': Spica can list filesystem contents on targeted systems. Software 'Kazuar': Kazuar finds a specified directory, lists the files and metadata about those files. Software 'XAgentOSX': XAgentOSX contains the readFiles function to return a detailed listing (sometimes recursive) of a specified directory. XAgentOSX contains the showBackupIosFolder function to check for IOS device backups by running ls -la ~/Library/Application\ Support/MobileSync/Backup/. Software 'HermeticWiper': HermeticWiper can enumerate common folders such as My Documents, Desktop, and AppData. Software 'Cyclops Blink': Cyclops Blink can use the Linux API `statvfs` to enumerate the current working directory. Software 'FIVEHANDS': FIVEHANDS has the ability to enumerate files on a compromised host in order to encrypt files with specific extensions. Software 'Black Basta': Black Basta can enumerate specific files for encryption. Software 'Cobalt Strike': Cobalt Strike can explore files on a compromised system. Software 'DarkWatchman': DarkWatchman has the ability to enumerate file and folder names. Software 'Industroyer': Industroyer’s data wiper component enumerates specific files on all the Windows drives. Software 'Brave Prince': Brave Prince gathers file and directory information from the victim’s machine. Software 'Havoc': The Havoc interface can display a file explorer view of the compromised host. Software 'OceanSalt': OceanSalt can extract drive information from the endpoint and search files on the system. Software 'NETWIRE': NETWIRE has the ability to search for files on the compromised host. Software 'Manjusaka': Manjusaka can gather information about specific files on the victim system. Software 'TINYTYPHON': TINYTYPHON searches through the drive containing the OS, then all drive letters C through to Z, for documents matching certain extensions. Software 'Octopus': Octopus can collect information on the Windows directory and searches for compressed RAR files on the host. Software 'SUNSPOT': SUNSPOT enumerated the Orion software Visual Studio solution directory path. Software 'Zox': Zox can enumerate files on a compromised host. Software 'Elise': A variant of Elise executes dir C:\progra~1 when initially run. Software 'Bisonal': Bisonal can retrieve a file listing from the system. Software 'MiniDuke': MiniDuke can enumerate local drives. Software 'Conti': Conti can discover files on a local system. Software 'SLOTHFULMEDIA': SLOTHFULMEDIA can enumerate files and directories. Software 'ADVSTORESHELL': ADVSTORESHELL can list files and directories. Software 'FinFisher': FinFisher enumerates directories and scans for certain files. Software 'Exbyte': Exbyte enumerates all document files on an infected machine, then creates a summary of these items including filename and directory location prior to exfiltration to cloud hosting services. Software 'Pcexter': Pcexter has the ability to search for files in specified directories. Software 'LoFiSe': LoFiSe can monitor the file system to identify files less than 6.4 MB in size with file extensions including .doc, .docx, .xls, .xlsx, .ppt, .pptx, .pdf, .rtf, .tif, .odt, .ods, .odp, .eml, and .msg. Software 'Avenger': Avenger has the ability to browse files in directories such as Program Files and the Desktop. Software 'COATHANGER': COATHANGER will survey the contents of system files during installation. Software 'CharmPower': CharmPower can enumerate drives and list the contents of the C: drive on a victim's computer. Software 'REvil': REvil has the ability to identify specific files and directories that are not to be encrypted. Software 'LightSpy': LightSpy uses the `NSFileManager` to move, create and delete files. LightSpy can also use the assembly `bt` instruction to determine a file's executable permissions. Software 'Gelsemium': Gelsemium can retrieve data from specific Windows directories, as well as open random files as part of Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion. Software 'Proxysvc': Proxysvc lists files in directories. Software 'LockBit 2.0': LockBit 2.0 can exclude files associated with core system functions from encryption. Software 'GravityRAT': GravityRAT collects the volumes mapped on the system, and also steals files with the following extensions: .docx, .doc, .pptx, .ppt, .xlsx, .xls, .rtf, and .pdf. Software 'Bandook': Bandook has a command to list files on a system. Software 'Trojan.Karagany': Trojan.Karagany can enumerate files and directories on a compromised host. Software '3PARA RAT': 3PARA RAT has a command to retrieve metadata for files on disk as well as a command to list the current working directory. Software 'DUSTTRAP': DUSTTRAP can enumerate files and directories. Software 'NotPetya': NotPetya searches for files ending with dozens of different file extensions prior to encryption. Software 'ThreatNeedle': ThreatNeedle can obtain file and directory information. Software 'BoomBox': BoomBox can search for specific files and directories on a machine. Software 'Samurai': Samurai can use a specific module for file enumeration. Software 'jRAT': jRAT can browse file systems. Software 'Clop': Clop has searched folders and subfolders for files to encrypt. Software 'GoldenSpy': GoldenSpy has included a program "ExeProtector", which monitors for the existence of GoldenSpy on the infected system and redownloads if necessary. Software 'OutSteel': OutSteel can search for specific file extensions, including zipped files. Software 'FYAnti': FYAnti can search the C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\ directory for files of a specified size. Software 'BADFLICK': BADFLICK has searched for files on the infected host. Software 'CaddyWiper': CaddyWiper can enumerate all files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'Stuxnet': Stuxnet uses a driver to scan for specific filesystem driver objects. Software 'CHOPSTICK': An older version of CHOPSTICK has a module that monitors all mounted volumes for files with the extensions .doc, .docx, .pgp, .gpg, .m2f, or .m2o. Software 'Zebrocy': Zebrocy searches for files that are 60mb and less and contain the following extensions: .doc, .docx, .xls, .xlsx, .ppt, .pptx, .exe, .zip, and .rar. Zebrocy also runs the echo %APPDATA% command to list the contents of the directory. Zebrocy can obtain the current execution path as well as perform drive enumeration. Software 'AvosLocker': AvosLocker has searched for files and directories on a compromised network. Software 'PLEAD': PLEAD has the ability to list drives and files on the compromised host. Software 'Okrum': Okrum has used DriveLetterView to enumerate drive information. Software 'ObliqueRAT': ObliqueRAT has the ability to recursively enumerate files on an infected endpoint. Software 'BBSRAT': BBSRAT can list file and directory information. Software 'StrongPity': StrongPity can parse the hard drive on a compromised host to identify specific file extensions. Software 'Troll Stealer': Troll Stealer can enumerate and collect items from local drives and folders. Software 'QuietSieve': QuietSieve can search files on the target host by extension, including doc, docx, xls, rtf, odt, txt, jpg, pdf, rar, zip, and 7z. Software 'ROKRAT': ROKRAT has the ability to gather a list of files and directories on the infected system. Software 'ODAgent': ODAgent can identify the current working directory. Software 'HotCroissant': HotCroissant has the ability to retrieve a list of files in a given directory as well as drives and drive types. Software 'Raspberry Robin': Raspberry Robin will check to see if the initial executing script is located on the user's Desktop as an anti-analysis check. Software 'Peppy': Peppy can identify specific files for exfiltration. Software 'Psylo': Psylo has commands to enumerate all storage devices and to find all files that start with a particular string. Software 'Doki': Doki has resolved the path of a process PID to use as a script argument. Software 'ZIPLINE': ZIPLINE can find and append specific files on Ivanti Connect Secure VPNs based upon received commands. Software 'BlackMould': BlackMould has the ability to find files on the targeted system. Software 'CreepyDrive': CreepyDrive can specify the local file path to upload files from. Software 'POWRUNER': POWRUNER may enumerate user directories on a victim. Software 'KeyBoy': KeyBoy has a command to launch a file browser or explorer on the system. Software 'Dtrack': Dtrack can list files on available disk volumes. Software 'BACKSPACE': BACKSPACE allows adversaries to search for files. Software 'DDKONG': DDKONG lists files on the victim’s machine. Software 'Denis': Denis has several commands to search directories for files. Software 'LunarMail': LunarMail can search its staging directory for output files it has produced. Software 'INC Ransomware': INC Ransomware can receive command line arguments to encrypt specific files and directories. Software 'OSX/Shlayer': OSX/Shlayer has used the command appDir="$(dirname $(dirname "$currentDir"))" and $(dirname "$(pwd -P)") to construct installation paths. Software 'Heyoka Backdoor': Heyoka Backdoor has the ability to search the compromised host for files. Software 'Cryptoistic': Cryptoistic can scan a directory to identify files for deletion. Software 'DarkGate': Some versions of DarkGate search for the hard-coded folder C:\Program Files\e Carte Bleue. Software 'RansomHub': RansomHub has the ability to only encrypt specific files. Software 'CORALDECK': CORALDECK searches for specified files. Software 'HTTPBrowser': HTTPBrowser is capable of listing files, folders, and drives on a victim. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has searched for files within the victim environment for encryption and exfiltration. Medusa Ransomware has also identified files associated with remote management services. Software 'SameCoin': SameCoin can list all system files and can avoid wiping specific directories such as Program Files, Windows, and Users. Software 'ChChes': ChChes collects the victim's %TEMP% directory path and version of Internet Explorer. Software 'Pasam': Pasam creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can retrieve lists of files. Software 'metaMain': metaMain can recursively enumerate files in an operator-provided directory. Software 'RainyDay': RainyDay can use a file exfiltration tool to collect recently changed files with specific extensions. Software 'InvisiMole': InvisiMole can list information about files in a directory and recently opened or used documents. InvisiMole can also search for specific files by supplied file mask. Software 'Gold Dragon': Gold Dragon lists the directories for Desktop, program files, and the user’s recently accessed files. Software 'Playcrypt': Playcrypt can avoid encrypting files with a .PLAY, .exe, .msi, .dll, .lnk, or .sys file extension. Software 'AppleSeed': AppleSeed has the ability to search for .txt, .ppt, .hwp, .pdf, and .doc files in specified directories. Software 'FoggyWeb': FoggyWeb's loader can check for the FoggyWeb backdoor .pri file on a compromised AD FS server. Software 'Cannon': Cannon can obtain victim drive information as well as a list of folders in C:\Program Files. Software 'KGH_SPY': KGH_SPY can enumerate files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'Mispadu': Mispadu searches for various filesystem paths to determine what banking applications are installed on the victim’s machine. Software 'WastedLocker': WastedLocker can enumerate files and directories just prior to encryption. Software 'NDiskMonitor': NDiskMonitor can obtain a list of all files and directories as well as logical drives. Software 'Ixeshe': Ixeshe can list file and directory information. Software 'Akira _v2': Akira _v2 can target specific files and folders for encryption. Software 'Backdoor.Oldrea': Backdoor.Oldrea collects information about available drives, default browser, desktop file list, My Documents, Internet history, program files, and root of available drives. It also searches for ICS-related software files. Software 'Derusbi': Derusbi is capable of obtaining directory, file, and drive listings. Software 'Siloscape': Siloscape searches for the Kubernetes config file and other related files using a regular expression. Software 'StrifeWater': StrifeWater can enumerate files on a compromised host. Software 'MobileOrder': MobileOrder has a command to upload to its C2 server information about files on the victim mobile device, including SD card size, installed app list, SMS content, contacts, and calling history. Software 'BLUELIGHT': BLUELIGHT can enumerate files and collect associated metadata. Software 'ccf32': ccf32 can parse collected files to identify specific file extensions. Software 'TajMahal': TajMahal has the ability to index files from drives, user profiles, and removable drives. Software 'Prestige': Prestige can traverse the file system to discover files to encrypt by identifying specific extensions defined in a hardcoded list. Software 'Gomir': Gomir collects information about directory and file structures, including total number of subdirectories, total number of files, and total size of files on infected systems. Software 'SOUNDBITE': SOUNDBITE is capable of enumerating and manipulating files and directories. Software 'Hydraq': Hydraq creates a backdoor through which remote attackers can check for the existence of files, including its own components, as well as retrieve a list of logical drives. Software 'Qilin': Qilin can exclude specific directories and files from encryption. Software 'StreamEx': StreamEx has the ability to enumerate drive types. Software 'Taidoor': Taidoor can search for specific files. Software 'BLINDINGCAN': BLINDINGCAN can search, read, write, move, and execute files. Software 'LODEINFO': LODEINFO has the ability to designate specific files and folders to encryption. Software 'TrickBot': TrickBot searches the system for all of the following file extensions: .avi, .mov, .mkv, .mpeg, .mpeg4, .mp4, .mp3, .wav, .ogg, .jpeg, .jpg, .png, .bmp, .gif, .tiff, .ico, .xlsx, and .zip. It can also obtain browsing history, cookies, and plug-in information. Software 'Mafalda': Mafalda can search for files and directories. Software 'BeaverTail': BeaverTail has searched for .ldb and .log files stored in browser extension directories for collection and exfiltration. Software 'LookBack': LookBack can retrieve file listings from the victim machine. Software 'Aria-body': Aria-body has the ability to gather metadata from a file and to search for file and directory names. Software 'SplatCloak': SplatCloak has used Windows API to identify files associated with Windows Defender and Kaspersky. Software 'Kasidet': Kasidet has the ability to search for a given filename on a victim. Software 'Ramsay': Ramsay can collect directory and file lists. Software 'IceApple': The IceApple Directory Lister module can list information about files and directories including creation time, last write time, name, and size. Software 'Action RAT': Action RAT has the ability to collect drive and file information on an infected machine. Software 'Rising Sun': Rising Sun can enumerate information about files from the infected system, including file size, attributes, creation time, last access time, and write time. Rising Sun can enumerate the compilation timestamp of Windows executable files. Software 'SPACESHIP': SPACESHIP identifies files and directories for collection by searching for specific file extensions or file modification time. Software 'Penquin': Penquin can use the command code do_vslist to send file names, size, and status to C2. Software 'Machete': Machete produces file listings in order to search for files to be exfiltrated. Software 'ROADSWEEP': ROADSWEEP can enumerate files on infected devices and avoid encrypting files with .exe, .dll, .sys, .lnk, or . lck extensions. Software 'RTM': RTM can check for specific files and directories associated with virtualization and malware analysis. Software 'KONNI': A version of KONNI searches for filenames created with a previous version of the malware, suggesting different versions targeted the same victims and the versions may work together. Software 'RedLeaves': RedLeaves can enumerate and search for files and directories. Software 'WarzoneRAT': WarzoneRAT can enumerate directories on a compromise host. Software 'AcidPour': AcidPour can identify specific files and directories within the Linux operating system corresponding with storage devices for follow-on wiping activity, similar to AcidRain. Software 'BRICKSTORM': BRICKSTORM has identified specific files and directories within targeted hosts and systems for modification, execution, collection and exfiltration. Software 'AcidRain': AcidRain identifies specific files and directories in the Linux operating system associated with storage devices. Software 'Saint Bot': Saint Bot can search a compromised host for specific files. Software 'Nebulae': Nebulae can list files and directories on a compromised host. Software 'FruitFly': FruitFly looks for specific files and file types. Software 'XCSSET': XCSSET has used `mdfind` to enumerate a list of apps known to grant screen sharing permissions and leverages a module to run the command `ls -la ~/Desktop`. Software 'Azorult': Azorult can recursively search for files in folders and collects files from the desktop with certain extensions. Software 'PACEMAKER': PACEMAKER can parse `/proc/"process_name"/cmdline` to look for the string `dswsd` within the command line. Software 'Remexi': Remexi searches for files on the system. Software 'SombRAT': SombRAT can execute enum to enumerate files in storage on a compromised system. Software 'Cheerscrypt': Cheerscrypt can search for log and VMware-related files with .log, .vmdk, .vmem, .vswp, and .vmsn extensions. Software 'Prikormka': A module in Prikormka collects information about the paths, size, and creation time of files with specific file extensions, but not the actual content of the file. Software 'Raccoon Stealer': Raccoon Stealer identifies target files and directories for collection based on a configuration file. Software 'MacMa': MacMa can search for a specific file on the compromised computer and can enumerate files in Desktop, Downloads, and Documents folders. Software 'Royal': Royal can identify specific files and directories to exclude from the encryption process. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo has searched the Program Files directories for specific folders and has searched for strings related to its mutexes. Software 'Megazord': Megazord can ignore specified directories for encryption. Software 'BlackCat': BlackCat can enumerate files for encryption. Software 'Smoke Loader': Smoke Loader recursively searches through directories for files. Software 'UPPERCUT': UPPERCUT has the capability to gather the victim's current directory. Software 'BADNEWS': BADNEWS identifies files with certain extensions from USB devices, then copies them to a predefined directory. Software 'Babuk': Babuk has the ability to enumerate files on a targeted system. Software 'China Chopper': China Chopper's server component can list directory contents. Software 'FunnyDream': FunnyDream can identify files with .doc, .docx, .ppt, .pptx, .xls, .xlsx, and .pdf extensions and specific timestamps for collection. Software 'Uroburos': Uroburos can search for specific files on a compromised system. Software 'TYPEFRAME': TYPEFRAME can search directories for files on the victim’s machine. Group 'Volt Typhoon': Volt Typhoon has enumerated directories containing vulnerability testing and cyber related content and facilities data such as construction drawings.Volt Typhoon used KV Botnet Activity to build intermediate communication chains between operators and victims, such as identified access to victims in Guam.KV Botnet Activity gathers a list of filenames from the following locations during execution of the final botnet stage: \/usr\/sbin\/, \/usr\/bin\/, \/sbin\/, \/pfrm2.0\/bin\/, \/usr\/local\/bin\/. The actors also searched for documents based on a specific date range and attempted to identify all installed software on a victim. Group 'Gamaredon Group': Gamaredon Group macros can scan for Microsoft Word and Excel files to inject with additional malicious macros. Gamaredon Group has also used its backdoors to automatically list interesting files (such as Office documents) found on a system. Gamaredon Group has also identified directory trees, folders and files on the compromised host. Group 'APT38': APT38 have enumerated files and directories, or searched in specific locations within a compromised host. Group 'Inception': Inception used a file listing plugin to collect information about file and directories both on local and remote drives. During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace enumerated file system details in compromised environments. Group 'RedCurl': RedCurl has searched for and collected files on local and network drives. Group 'Velvet Ant': Velvet Ant has enumerated local files and folders on victim devices. Group 'Chimera': Chimera has utilized multiple commands to identify data of interest in file and directory listings. Group 'admin@338': admin@338 actors used the following commands after exploiting a machine with LOWBALL malware to obtain information about files and directories: dir c:\ >> %temp%\download dir "c:\Documents and Settings" >> %temp%\download dir "c:\Program Files\" >> %temp%\download dir d:\ >> %temp%\download Group 'Ke3chang': Ke3chang uses command-line interaction to search files and directories. Group 'Sandworm Team': Sandworm Team has enumerated files on a compromised host. Group 'Sidewinder': Sidewinder has used malware to collect information on files and directories. Group 'Scattered Spider': Scattered Spider Spider enumerates a target organization for files and directories of interest, including source code, user provisioning, MFA device registration, network diagrams, and shared credentials in documents or spreadsheets. Group 'Aoqin Dragon': Aoqin Dragon has run scripts to identify file formats including Microsoft Word. Group 'Tropic Trooper': Tropic Trooper has monitored files' modified time. Group 'Dark Caracal': Dark Caracal collected file listings of all default Windows directories. Group 'MuddyWater': MuddyWater has used malware that checked if the ProgramData folder had folders or files with the keywords "Kasper," "Panda," or "ESET." Group 'Magic Hound': Magic Hound malware can list a victim's logical drives and the type, as well the total/free space of the fixed devices. Other malware can list a directory's contents. Group 'TeamTNT': TeamTNT has used a script that checks `/proc/*/environ` for environment variables related to AWS. Group 'Lotus Blossom': Lotus Blossom has used commands such as `dir` to examine the local filesystem of victim machines. Group 'LuminousMoth': LuminousMoth has used malware that scans for files in the Documents, Desktop, and Download folders and in other drives. Group 'APT3': APT3 has a tool that looks for files and directories on the local file system. Group 'APT32': APT32's backdoor possesses the capability to list files and directories on a machine. Group 'Mustang Panda': Mustang Panda has searched the entire target system for DOC, DOCX, PPT, PPTX, XLS, XLSX, and PDF files. Group 'APT39': APT39 has used tools with the ability to search for files on a compromised host. Group 'ToddyCat': ToddyCat has run scripts to enumerate recently modified documents having either a .pdf, .doc, .docx, .xls or .xlsx extension. Group 'Lazarus Group': Lazarus Group malware can use a common function to identify target files by their extension, and some also enumerate files and directories, including a Destover-like variant that lists files and gathers information for all drives.During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group conducted word searches within documents on a compromised host in search of security and financial matters. Group 'Darkhotel': Darkhotel has used malware that searched for files with specific patterns. Group 'Play': Play has used the Grixba information stealer to list security files and processes. Group 'menuPass': menuPass has searched compromised systems for folders of interest including those related to HR, audit and expense, and meeting memos. Group 'Kimsuky': Kimsuky has the ability to enumerate all files and directories on an infected system. Kimsuky has used a custom script with a function called CreateFileList() that can scan all filesystem drives, prioritizing C:\Users, to locate files and file extensions of interest that ultimately generates a file called `FileList.txt` saved within the victims %TEMP% Directory that contains the findings and the respective pathways. Group 'HAFNIUM': HAFNIUM has searched file contents on a compromised host. Group 'Confucius': Confucius has used a file stealer that checks the Document, Downloads, Desktop, and Picture folders for documents and images with specific extensions. Group 'APT41': APT41 has executed file /bin/pwd on exploited victims, perhaps to return architecture related information. Group 'Winnti Group': Winnti Group has used a program named ff.exe to search for specific documents on compromised hosts. Group 'Winter Vivern': Winter Vivern delivered malicious JavaScript payloads capable of listing folders and emails in exploited email servers. Group 'Windigo': Windigo has used a script to check for the presence of files created by OpenSSH backdoors. Group 'APT18': APT18 can list files information for specific directories. Group 'APT5': APT5 has used the BLOODMINE utility to discover files with .css, .jpg, .png, .gif, .ico, .js, and .jsp extensions in Pulse Secure Connect logs. Group 'Fox Kitten': Fox Kitten has used WizTree to obtain network files and directory listings. Group 'Leafminer': Leafminer used a tool called MailSniper to search for files on the desktop and another utility called Sobolsoft to extract attachments from EML files. Group 'FIN13': FIN13 has used the Windows `dir` command to enumerate files and directories in a victim's network. Group 'Turla': Turla surveys a system upon check-in to discover files in specific locations on the hard disk %TEMP% directory, the current user's desktop, the Program Files directory, and Recent. Turla RPC backdoors have also searched for files matching the lPH*.dll pattern. Group 'BRONZE BUTLER': BRONZE BUTLER has collected a list of files from the victim and uploaded it to its C2 server, and then created a new list of specific files to steal. Group 'APT29': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 obtained information about the configured Exchange virtual directory using `Get-WebServicesVirtualDirectory`. Campaign 'C0015': During C0015, the threat actors conducted a file listing discovery against multiple hosts to ensure locker encryption was successful. Campaign 'Operation Honeybee': During Operation Honeybee, the threat actors used a malicious DLL to search for files with specific keywords. Campaign 'Operation CuckooBees': During Operation CuckooBees, the threat actors used `dir c:\\` to search for files. Campaign 'SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation': During SharePoint ToolShell Exploitation, threat actors leveraged commands to locate accessible file shares, backup paths, or SharePoint content. Campaign 'Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign': During the Anthropic AI-orchestrated Campaign, the adversary leveraged Claude Code to identify sensitive data within the victim environment for extraction. Campaign 'Salesforce Data Exfiltration': During Salesforce Data Exfiltration, threat actors queried customers' Salesforce environments to identify sensitive information for exfiltration. Campaign 'SolarWinds Compromise': During the SolarWinds Compromise, APT29 obtained information about the configured Exchange virtual directory using `Get-WebServicesVirtualDirectory`. Campaign 'Operation Wocao': During Operation Wocao, threat actors gathered a recursive directory listing to find files and directories of interest. Campaign 'Operation AkaiRyū': During Operation AkaiRyū, MirrorFace enumerated file system details in compromised environments. Campaign 'Operation Dream Job': During Operation Dream Job, Lazarus Group conducted word searches within documents on a compromised host in search of security and financial matters. Campaign 'Night Dragon': During Night Dragon, threat actors used zwShell to establish full remote control of the connected machine and browse the victim file system. Campaign '2025 Poland Wiper Attacks': During the 2025 Poland Wiper Attacks, the adversaries obtained the contents of users’ directories using `dir /s /b C:\Users` command. Campaign 'KV Botnet Activity': KV Botnet Activity gathers a list of filenames from the following locations during execution of the final botnet stage: \/usr\/sbin\/, \/usr\/bin\/, \/sbin\/, \/pfrm2.0\/bin\/, \/usr\/local\/bin\/.

T1518.001.name: Security Software Discovery
T1518.001.description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of security software, configurations, defensive tools, and sensors that are installed on a system or in a cloud environment. This may include things such as cloud monitoring agents and anti-virus. Adversaries may use the information from [Security Software Discovery](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1518/001) during automated discovery to shape follow-on behaviors, including whether or not the adversary fully infects the target and/or attempts specific actions. Example commands that can be used to obtain security software information are [netsh](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0108), reg query with [Reg](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0075), dir with [cmd](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0106), and [Tasklist](https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0057), but other indicators of discovery behavior may be more specific to the type of software or security system the adversary is looking for. It is becoming more common to see macOS malware perform checks for LittleSnitch and KnockKnock software. Adversaries may also utilize the [Cloud API](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/009) to discover cloud-native security software installed on compute infrastructure, such as the AWS CloudWatch agent, Azure VM Agent, and Google Cloud Monitor agent. These agents may collect metrics and logs from the VM, which may be centrally aggregated in a cloud-based monitoring platform.
T1518.001.detections:
Detection Strategy: Security Software Discovery Across Platforms
- Analytic 'Analytic 0048': Adversary executes commands to enumerate installed antivirus, EDR, or firewall agents using WMI, registry queries, and built-in tools (e.g., tasklist, netsh, sc query). Correlated with elevated process privileges or scripting engine usage.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0049': Adversary runs discovery commands such as `ps aux`, `systemctl status`, or `cat /etc/init.d/` to enumerate security software or services. Often occurs alongside privilege escalation or bash script execution.
- Analytic 'Analytic 0050': Adversary attempts to detect monitoring agents such as Little Snitch, KnockKnock, or other system daemons via process listing (`ps -e`), application folder checks, and system extension listing.
T1518.001.procedures: Software 'Tasklist': Tasklist can be used to enumerate security software currently running on a system by process name of known products. Software 'Brute Ratel C4': Brute Ratel C4 can detect EDR userland hooks. Software 'Empire': Empire can enumerate antivirus software on the target. Software 'Pacu': Pacu can enumerate AWS security services, including WAF rules and GuardDuty detectors. Software 'netsh': netsh can be used to discover system firewall settings. Software 'SILENTTRINITY': SILENTTRINITY can determine if an anti-virus product is installed through the resolution of the service's virtual SID. Software 'Raspberry Robin': Raspberry Robin attempts to identify security software running on the victim machine, such as BitDefender, Avast, and Kaspersky. Software 'Clop': Clop can search for processes with antivirus and antimalware product names. Software 'ABK': ABK has the ability to identify the installed anti-virus product on the compromised host. Software 'PUBLOAD': PUBLOAD has identified AV products on an infected host using the following command: `WMIC /Node:localhost /Namespace:\\root\SecurityCenter2 Path AntiVirusProduct Get displayName /Format:List`. Software 'Metamorfo': Metamorfo collects a list of installed antivirus software from the victim’s system. Software 'SplatCloak': SplatCloak has identified drivers of AV solutions by searching for related filenames, keywords and signed certificates. Software 'TONESHELL': TONESHELL has checked for the presence of ESET antivirus applications `ekrn.exe` and `egui.exe`. Software 'QakBot': QakBot can identify the installed antivirus product on a targeted system. Software 'Micropsia': Micropsia searches for anti-virus software and firewall products installed on the victim’s machine using WMI. Software 'Crimson': Crimson contains a command to collect information about anti-virus software on the victim. Software 'Zeus Panda': Zeus Panda checks to see if anti-virus, anti-spyware, or firewall products are installed in the victim’s environment. Software 'MuddyViper': MuddyViper has the ability to check for a specified list of security tools in the compromised environment. Software 'Medusa Ransomware': Medusa Ransomware has the capability to detect security solutions for termination or deletion within the victim device using hard-coded lists of strings containing security product executables. Software 'Amadey': Amadey has checked for a variety of antivirus products. Software 'Comnie': Comnie attempts to detect several anti-virus products. Software 'Bazar': Bazar can identify the installed antivirus engine. Software 'RedLine Stealer': RedLine Stealer has identified installed antivirus software on the system.
